segments.s208-g161: ز here is the poetic preposition 'ز' (= 'from' or 'because of') introducing the cause. گفتار بدگوی is a genitive/attributive sequence: گفتار ('speech') followed by بدگوی functioning as an agentive adjective/noun 'the slanderer' — hence 'the slanderer's speech' or 'slanderous speech'. وز is the fused poetic sequence (و + ز) linking and adding 'and/of' to the next phrase (i.e. 'and (concerning)'); نام و ننگ is a coordinated noun phrase 'name and shame' (reputation and dishonor) that complements the causal sense begun by ز.
segments.s208-g162: جهان is fronted as the thing affected ('the world' or 'this life'); کرد is the simple past of کردن 'to make/cause' (3rd person) so جهان کرد = 'made the world...'. بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon/for himself' indicating who is afflicted. تار و تنگ is an idiomatic paired adjective (tightly linked by و) meaning 'narrow, cramped, constricted' that serves as the result/complement of کرد ('made the world narrow and cramped for himself').
segments.s208-g113: شوم is an optative/first‑person form meaning “may I become” or a hortatory/future sense “I will be.” کین (here) is a noun meaning “vengeance/hatred” placed before the direct object for emphasis. این ننگ بازآورم is a verbless unit where بازآورم (1st‑person present/subjunctive) governs این ننگ as its object: literally “I (shall) bring back/restore this shame” — i.e. “avenge this disgrace.” Word order is typical classical Persian: optative verb → object/emphatic noun phrase → verbal predicate.
segments.s208-g114: This clause is a compact verbal sentence: سر شیب را is the object phrase (سر = “head” in anatomical/figurative sense; شیب qualifies it as ‘sloped/bowed head’) marked by را, and برفراز آورم is the verb phrase with the locative/ adverbial برفراز (“on high”) before آورم (1st‑person present/subjunctive/intentional) meaning “I will bring/raise.” Together: “I will raise the (bowed) head aloft.”
segments.s208-g115: همه رنج لشکر is a genitive group “all the army’s hardships.” به تن is a prepositional phrase literally “to/onto the body” functioning idiomatically as “upon myself/physically.” برنهم is a 1st‑person verb here meaning “I will put on / assume / endure” (an older/classical form); the whole clause reads “I will put all the army’s suffering upon my body” (i.e. I will endure it).
segments.s208-g116: This is a paired conditional phrasing: اگر جان ستانم اگر جان دهم uses اگر (“if”) twice to present two contrasted possibilities. ستانم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of ستاندن “to take/pluck” meaning here “if I take (a life).” جان دهم uses دهم (“I give”) with جان “life” to mean “if I give my life.” The structure expresses willingness in either case.
segments.s208-g117: از این پس is a temporal phrase “from now on / henceforth.” به تخت و کله is the coordinated object “to/at throne and (lit.) head/crown.” ننگرم is the verb (1st‑person) built on نگر/نگاه meaning “to look at / regard”; here it functions negatively in sense of not caring for those things from now on (the negative force is supplied by the following clause with جز … نبیند). Word order is temporal → object → verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s208-g118: جز از ترگ رومی sets up an exception: “except for a Roman rag.” نبیند is a 3rd‑person form of دیدن (here used as a future/gnomic ‘will see / shall behold’), and سرم is the possessed noun “my head” (my sight). The clause means literally “my head will see nothing except a Roman rag,” i.e. he will behold nothing but humiliation (the rag).
segments.s208-g119: ز گفتار او is an ablative/causal phrase “from/because of his speech.” شاد شد is the simple past/resultative “became glad,” and شهریار is the subject following the verb (common in epic/style): “the king became glad from his words.” The verb شد is the simple past here (he became).
segments.s208-g120: دلش تازه شد is “his heart became fresh/new” (دل + possessive ش). چون گل اندر بهار is a simile placed after the verb: چون = “like,” گل = “a flower,” اندر بهار = “in spring.” Overall a standard simile: “his heart grew fresh as a flower in spring.” Note the adjective تازه follows the possessed noun and precedes شد.
segments.s208-g137: شما را is the ethical/object phrase ('for you' / 'you' as the one affected) marked by the object marker را before the predicate. شادمانیست is a poetic contraction of شادمانی است ('it is joy'), so the clause reads literally 'to you it is all joy'; رای follows as the noun 'opinion/consent' (the sense in which people are inclined). In other words: 'Everyone's opinion/feeling is joy toward you.'
segments.s208-g138: نجنبد is a negative present form ('does not move/does not stir') and is strengthened by the enclitic/early classical particle همی marking ongoing or emphatic present action. به کینه is an ablative/causal phrase 'by/from hatred' (به here = 'by/through'), ز جای literally 'from the place' so دل ز جای نجنبد means 'the heart will not move from its place' — i.e. hatred will not make the heart stir.
segments.s208-g139: دلیران همه is the plural subject 'all the brave'. دست کرده here uses the perfective participle کرده with دست to form the idiom 'دست کردن (به ...)' = 'to take up (something) / set one's hand to' and is followed by the verbal root کش (short for کشیدن) introduced by به to indicate the action they take up: 'have set their hand to drawing (a weapon)'.
segments.s208-g140: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before / in the presence of'. خداوند here is an honorific meaning 'lord / sovereign' rather than the monotheistic God, and خورشیدفش is an epithet or proper-name-like epithet 'the sun‑bright / radiant (one)'. Together they specify before whom the action is done.
segments.s208-g141: همه همگنان is 'all the companions/peers'. خاک دادند بوس is an idiom: دادند (gave) plus the noun بوس ('a kiss') with خاک as its object — literally 'gave the earth a kiss', i.e. 'kissed the ground' or prostrated themselves. دادند is the simple past 'they gave'.
segments.s208-g142: چو here means 'as / like' and introduces examples: pairs of proper names are listed (رهام و گرگین, چو گودرز و طوس) — the second طوس is the hero Tūs (not the city). The sequence is a straightforward simile/list: 'as Roham and Gorgin, as Goudarz and Tous.'
segments.s208-g143: چو ('like') introduces another comparison. خراد here functions adjectivally/nominatively ('Khurad' / 'the wise') and با marks accompaniment 'with'. زنگه is used figuratively here (literally 'peal' or 'ring'), modifying شاوران ('counsellors, advisers') — together giving a poetic epithet 'with the ringing/admonishing counsellors'. The whole phrase is a poetic listing of heroes and their retinues.
segments.s208-g144: دگر here means 'other' / 'the others' and introduces further names: بیژن و گیو و کنداوران (Bijan, Giv, and Kondavaran). The conjunction و links the names; the final name is presented as a noun in apposition (here treated as a proper name in context though the provided senses are non‑proper).
segments.s208-g65: تو خواهشگری کن is an imperative construction: تو (you) is the addressee, خواهشگری is a verbal-noun/gerund or noun 'entreaty/petition' used as the object of the imperative, and کن is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'do/make'. که introduces the reason/explanatory clause 'for' (because). شاه is the subject of the explanatory clause. Note: برناست here is an adjectival/predicative phrase attached to شاه (شاه برناست = 'the king is young'), but the lemma given in the data doesn't match the poetic sense; treat برناست as 'is young' functioning as the predicate of that clause.
segments.s208-g66: مگر introduces an exception/conditional here meaning 'unless'. The verb بپیچد is a present/subjunctive form (prefix بـ + پیچد) used for a potential/change-of-mind action 'turns' or 'is turned'. سر is the semantic subject meaning 'head' or 'mind/intent', and ز + کین marks cause 'because of/through (the) hatred/vengeance' of سپاه (the host/army). The clause as a whole is 'unless the head turns because of the army's hatred.'
segments.s208-g67: نه begins a negative coordinate/thesis ('nor'/'not'), followed by a nominal phrase: فرزند 'son/child' as head, کاوس‌کی is a genitive attributive 'of Kāvus', and ریونیز appears as a proper-name or epithet attached to that noun (here treated as an identifying name). The phrase names the person who is then described in the next segment.
segments.s208-g68: به جنگ اندرون gives the locative/context: به + جنگ + اندرون = 'in/into the inner battle' (به used as 'into/in'). کشته شد is a passive past: کشته (killed) + شد (auxiliary 'was'), so 'was killed'. زار is an adjectival comment 'wretched' or 'pitiful' modifying the slain person; نیز at the end is the enclitic 'also/likewise', linking this fact to what came before.
segments.s208-g69: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. کهتر پسر بود is a simple past copular clause: کهتر 'inferior/younger' modifying پسر 'son', and بود is the past copula 'was'. و links a second predicate: پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' describing the same referent.
segments.s208-g70: دریغ is an interjectional/exclamatory noun 'pity/alas' expressing regret. آنچنان is an intensifier 'so/such', and خسرو ماهروی is a nominal phrase 'Khosrow the moon-faced' (خسرو as a proper name; ماهروی an epithet meaning 'moon-faced/beautiful'). The whole segment laments the described fate of that Khosrow.
segments.s208-g71: چنین است is a copular formula 'thus is/so is'. انجام و فرجام جنگ are two nouns in apposition meaning 'the result and the end/fate of war' (جنگ governed as the domain of those nouns). Word order is typical Persian: predicate followed by the governing genitive.
segments.s208-g72: یکی ... یکی is a rhythmic correlative 'one ... another/one ... one', where یابد (3rd sing. present/subjunctive or simple present in classical style) means 'attains/receives'. تاج یابد = 'one attains the crown'; یکی گور تنگ = 'another (finds) the narrow grave' — گور تنگ is a descriptive compound 'narrow grave'. The sentence states the contrasting fates resulting from war.
segments.s208-g145: که‌ای here is a poetic vocative particle equivalent to “O” addressing someone directly; it’s not the relative conjunction 'که' in its usual senses. شاه نیک‌اختر و شیردل is a string of titles/epithets in apposition: شاه (king) + نیک‌اختر (an epithet/name) + و (and) + شیردل (lion‑hearted). The sequence is nominal and serves to address and praise the addressee before the verb clause that follows.
segments.s208-g146: ببرده is a perfect/perfective participial form meaning 'has taken/removed' and here describes the addressee's achievement; Persian poetry often omits an explicit relative pronoun, so this participle links back to the vocative. ز marks the source/cause ('from' or 'of'), شیران is used metaphorically for 'the brave' (not literal beasts), به + شمشیر uses به in the instrumental sense 'by/with' and دل ('heart') specifies the kind of sword (i.e. courage, 'sword of the heart').
segments.s208-g147: همه یک به یک is an idiom meaning 'all, one by one' (یک... به... = 'one by one'); پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'in your presence/before you'. بنده‌ایم is the copular present 'we are servants' — here a humble self‑reference (polite formula). Word order is subject/adverbial → prepositional phrase → predicate, normal in Persian poetic style.
segments.s208-g148: ز has the causal sense 'because of' here. تشویر is a noun meaning 'adornment/ornamentation' or 'display' and governs the following noun خسرو, which here functions as 'Khosrow/the king' (title or name). سرافگنده‌ایم is a compound present meaning 'we are abashed/ashamed' (state/result). The clause thus gives the reason for their abject/humble stance before the king.
segments.s208-g149: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. جنگ فرمان دهد is a compact poetic order: جنگ (war) + فرمان (command) + دهد (gives). Classical Persian often permits object/phrase ordering before the verb; here the verb دهد is the present/subjunctive form used in conditionals. شهریار (at the end) is the subject 'the sovereign/king' who issues the command.
segments.s208-g150: همه is 'all/everyone' and سرفشانیم is a present plural describing the group's triumphant/exultant state ('we exult' / 'we toss our heads in triumph'); در کارزار is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the battle/field' specifying where that exultation will take place. The clause is the apodosis (result) of the preceding conditional.
segments.s208-g151: سپهدار is the agent 'the commander'. پس is an adverb 'then' marking sequence. گیو را is the definite object (را marks it) and پیش خواند is a compound where خواند here means 'called/summoned' and پیش means 'before (the sovereign)'; literal order places the object before the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s208-g152: به تخت indicates direction/place 'onto/to the throne' (به with a locative/into sense). گرانمایگان is a genitive/attributive noun 'the illustrious/the exalted (ones)', qualifying the throne; برنشاند is a transitive past form meaning 'placed/caused to sit' — together: 'he seated him on the illustrious throne.'
segments.s208-g41: This is an exclamatory nominal phrase. "دریغ" (alas/pity) frames the line; "آن" is a demonstrative determinative pointing to the following noun phrase, and "فرود" (here 'fall'/'downfall') names the event. The final repeated "دریغ" is a poetic refrain for emphasis rather than a separate clause.
segments.s208-g42: "که" introduces a relative/explanatory clause referring back to the fall. "با زور و دل بود" literally 'was with strength and heart' — i.e. 'he possessed strength and courage'; "بود" is the past copula. The second coordinated phrase "و با گرز و تیغ" names the weapons (mace and sword) involved; the whole clause contrasts the man's qualities with the violent means of his downfall.
segments.s208-g43: "به سان" is a simile meaning 'like' or 'as'; it introduces the comparison to "پدر" (father). "کشته شد" is a passive past construction ('was killed/slain'), and "بی‌گناه" follows the verb as a predicative adjective meaning 'innocent'.
segments.s208-g44: "به دست" is an agent phrase meaning 'by the hand of' (i.e., 'by the agency of'). "سپهدار من" is 'my commander' with the possessive pronoun after the noun (common in Persian). "با سپاه" adds accompaniment/instrument ('with an army'). Together they specify the agent and means of the killing.
segments.s208-g45: "به گیتی" is a locative phrase 'in the world'. The negative predicate "نباشد کم" (literally 'is not few') functions idiomatically to mean 'there is no lack' or 'there are many'. "از طوس کس" uses "از" to indicate the class 'of/from Tous' — 'people like Tous'. The word order places the negated verb before the quantifier for emphasis.
segments.s208-g46: "که" introduces a relative clause describing those people. "او" is the personal pronoun 'he'. The phrase "از در بند چاهست" is an older/poetic compression corresponding to "در بندِ چاه است" meaning 'is bound in the bonds/prison of a well' (i.e. trapped or imprisoned in a pit). The final "و بس" is a terse addition meaning 'and that is enough' or 'and that's all', closing the remark.
segments.s208-g47: The correlative negative construction "نه ... و نه ..." ('neither ... nor ...') is used. "در سرش مغز" and "در تنش رگ" are locative noun phrases literally 'in his head (there is) brain' and 'in his body (there is) vein' with the copula omitted (understood). The meaning is figurative: he lacks sense/courage.
segments.s208-g48: The exclamatory "چه ... چه ..." frames two parallel complaints: "چه طوس فرومایه پیشم" ('what a base/low-born Tous before me' — "پیشم" = 'in my presence'/'before me'), and "چه سگ" ('what a dog!') as an insult. "فرومایه" is an adjective denouncing social/moral standing; "سگ" is used metaphorically as a strong insult.
segments.s208-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army'. بیامد is a past singular verb 'came'; together چو + verb make a temporal clause. به راه چرم is a locative phrase: به + راه = 'to/along the road of', with چرم functioning as the name of that road (a place-name) modifying راه.
segments.s208-g2: کلات is the subject (a place-name). از بر و زیر آب is a prepositional phrase describing how Kalat stood: از بر 'from/on (the) top' and زیر 'under' آب 'water' — i.e. water above and beneath; می‌م is an archaic/poetic present copula ('is' / 'there is'), here stating the state of Kalat.
segments.s208-g3: همی is a durative adverb meaning 'continually/still'. یاد کردند is a past plural construction 'they remembered / recalled' (کردند is the past form of 'do' used with یاد). رزم فرود is the object: رزم 'battle' and فرود here refers to the opponent/name of the battle (proper name), so the phrase means 'the battle of/with Farud'.
segments.s208-g4: A simple nominal list 'پشیمانی و درد و تیمار' ('regret and pain and care/concern') followed by بود, the past copula 'was/there was', stating that these feelings existed or were present.
segments.s208-g5: همه is a universal quantifier 'every/all'. دل is 'heart(s)'. پر از درد is the idiom 'full of pain' (پر followed by از + noun). از بیم شاه uses از here in the causal sense 'because of / for fear of' (بیم شاه = 'fear of the king').
segments.s208-g6: دو دیده = 'both/the two eyes' (دو quantifies دیده). پر از خون = 'full of blood' (پر + از). و links the next noun phrase: تن پر گناه = 'the body full of sin' (تن 'body', پر گناه 'full of sin'). These are coordinated results/conditions introduced by the preceding causal phrase.
segments.s208-g7: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus'. شرمگین is an adjective 'ashamed', used predicatively with آمدند 'they came'. نزد شاه is the prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the king' (نزد + noun). آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came'.
segments.s208-g8: جگر خسته is a nominal phrase where جگر (literally 'liver') is used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling' and خسته is the adjective 'weary'. و links the next predicate 'پر گناه' ('full of sin'). آمدند repeats the past plural verb 'they came', carrying the main action for this coordinated descriptive string.
segments.s208-g89: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. برفروزد is the aorist/subjunctive-like form of برفروزیدن 'to kindle/illuminate' meaning 'he lights/brightens (it)'. نباشد شگفت is an inverted predicate: نباشد (negative of بودن) functions impersonally here ('there is not' / 'it is not'), and شگفت is the nominal predicate 'a wonder' — together 'it is no wonder'.
segments.s208-g90: جهانجوی را has the definite-object marker را after the noun 'the world-seeker / ambitious man' (here the target of the remark). کین نباید گرفت is a prohibition construction: نباید (impersonal 'one should not / it should not') + گرفت (take/seize) yields 'one must not take vengeance'; کین is the object 'vengeance'/'grudge'.
segments.s208-g91: بدو is 'to him' (به + او contracted in classical order) marking the indirect object 'to him'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here reported speech). خسرو is the speaker's name. که‌ای پهلوان is the vocative 'که ای پهلوان' = 'O champion' (classical/exclamatory address).
segments.s208-g92: دلم is دل + possessive suffix -م, 'my heart.' پر ز تیمار شد is a compound: پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + تیمار 'care/concern' + شد 'became' — 'became full of care/concern.' زان جوان = 'for/from that young man' (زان = 'from/for that'), giving the cause/target of the concern.
segments.s208-g93: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. پند تو is 'your counsel' (noun + possessive). داروی جان is a genitive phrase 'remedy of the soul' (داروی = دارو + -ی linking vowel), and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'your advice was a remedy for the soul.'
segments.s208-g94: وگرچه is the concessive 'although/even though.' دل is the subject 'the heart.' از introduces the cause 'from/with,' درد پیچان is a descriptive noun phrase 'wrenching/twisting pain' (پیچان modifying درد), and بود is the past 'was' — 'although the heart was in twisting pain.'
segments.s208-g95: به پوزش بیامد is literally 'came with apology' — به + noun expresses purpose/manner and بیامد is the past 'he came.' سپهدار طوس is the title and name (سپهدار 'commander' + طوس 'Tūs'), the subject performing the action.
segments.s208-g96: به پیش سپهبد means 'in the presence of / before the spahbed' (به پیش = 'before/in front of'). زمین داد بوس is literal 'gave a kiss to the earth' (زمین 'the ground/earth' as object, داد 'gave', بوس 'a kiss' — idiomatically 'he kissed the ground' as an act of obeisance).
segments.s208-g25: کنون is an adverbial particle meaning 'now' placed at the sentence start to time the action. کینه نو شد is a predicative phrase: کینه (noun) + نو (adjective) after the noun (typical Persian order) + شد (past of شدن ‘become/arise’), so literally 'a grudge became new' = 'a new grudge arose'. ز introduces the source ('from') and takes the following noun phrase as its object; here کین names the kind of source ('vengeance/hatred') and فرود is used as a proper name (possession/agent of that vengeance) rather than its literal lexical meanings.
segments.s208-g26: This clause places the object phrase before the subject and verb: سر طوس = 'the head of Tūs' (ezāfe is omitted, a common classical Persian feature), نوذر is the subject (proper name), and بباید is a modal/impersonal present-tense form meaning 'must/ought to'. درود is a verbal/nominal object meaning 'greeting' or 'paying respects' — so the construction reads 'Nowzar must give/say greeting to the head of Tous.'
segments.s208-g27: بگفتم is the first-person past 'I said' introducing reported speech; که is the complementizer 'that'. سوی + place names (کلات و چرم) is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward Kalāt and Chirm' — note چرم here functions as a place-name (proper noun) rather than the common noun 'leather', so the lexical sense listed does not apply.
segments.s208-g28: مروگر is the plural subject 'passers-by/wanderers'. فشانند is a present-tense plural verb 'they scatter/they shower'. بر (on/upon) + سر (head) marks the location 'upon the head' and درم is the direct object 'dirhams/coins' — the whole phrase means 'passers-by shower dirhams on (my) head.'
segments.s208-g29: کزان (کِ‏زان) is a compound prepositional/pronominal adverb meaning 'from that/that way'. ره is 'road/way' and together they set a source or provenance. فرودست here is an adjectival predicate 'lowly/subordinate' linked to the implied subject (elliptical). و با introduces an additional specification — مادرست functions as an adjectival/relational predicate 'is maternal/from the mother's side' (classical Persian often omits the copula 'است'), so the two predicates characterize the person by origin/status.
segments.s208-g30: This clause names an office/title and then gives two predicates. سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high military rank). نژادست is a predicate meaning 'is of (good) lineage' (نژاد + copula -ست), so 'is of noble birth'. و links the second predicate, کنداور (a descriptive noun 'one who searches/excavates/investigates' or figuratively 'resourceful investigator'), and است is the copula 'is' completing the clause. Word order places titles/subjects before predicates, with the copula often reduced or archaic (-ست).
segments.s208-g31: دامان here (literally 'hem/skirt') is used idiomatically for 'retinue/entourage' (a common poetic/metaphorical use); طوس is the possessive referent (Tūs). The two adjectives نامرد (unmanly/scoundrel) and ناهوشیار (heedless/unaware) follow the head noun as descriptive epithets; classical Persian commonly strings modifiers after the noun and omits the copula.
segments.s208-g32: چرا is the interrogative 'why' at the clause front. برد here functions as a past-tense verb 'took/led' (classical past of بردن) with لشکر ('army') as its direct object; the directional phrase به سوی حصار marks the goal 'toward the fortress.' (Note: the sense entries supplied for برد do not include the verbal meaning 'to take/lead', so that lexical sense is not represented in the provided list.)
segments.s208-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پاکیزه جان is an adjective + noun sequence meaning 'pure/purity of soul' (adjective precedes noun). فرود here is a noun 'descent' (literally 'the coming down' or 'coming forth') and takes ز as its object; the conjunction و then links that whole phrase to زرسپ ('and Zarasp'), so the prepositional force ('from') applies to both 'the descent of the pure soul' and 'Zarasp'.
segments.s208-g106: همی is the archaic/prosaic continuous/presumptive particle marking an ongoing or imminent action ('will/still'). برفروزم is first‑person singular present/future 'I illumine/set ablaze/shine' (بر‑ prefix + فروخت/فروز root pattern). چو is the comparative 'like/as', and آذرگشسپ is a proper name used as the standard of comparison ('like Azar‑Gashasp'). The implied subject of برفروزم is 'I'.
segments.s208-g107: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. من is the emphatic pronoun 'I'. گنهکارم is the predicative adjective with enclitic -م 'I am guilty/sinner' ('I am a sinner'). از انجمن literally 'because of/from the assembly' indicates the circumstance or locus in which the speaker is judged sinful.
segments.s208-g108: همی again marks a present/near‑future action. پیچم is first‑person singular of پیچیدن here with the sense 'I turn/withdraw/turn away' (an intransitive reflexive sense). از کرده خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from/of my own deeds' where کرده 'deed/done' is possessed/emphasized by خویشتن 'self/myself' — together 'my own actions'.
segments.s208-g109: به ویژه is a fixed phrase meaning 'especially' (به + ویژه). ز is the preposition 'from' attaching to the following proper name بهرام (Bahram). وز here should be read as the sequence و + ز ('and from') joining the next name ریونیز; in older poetry such concatenations occur. ریونیز is a proper name or epithet; the exact identification is rare, but grammatically it is the second object of 'from'.
segments.s208-g110: همی again marks continuing/present action. جان here means 'soul' or 'life'. خویشم is a possessive/ reflexive form ('my own / my inner disposition'); the lemma list gives 'خو' (habit/disposition), so read as 'my spirit/disposition'. نیاید is negative present 'does not come/appear'. بچیز is a negative idiom meaning 'to no avail / to nothing' (i.e., 'comes to nothing'). Together: 'my soul/disposition does not come to nothing.'
segments.s208-g111: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. شاه is 'the king'. خشنود is the predicate 'pleased'. گردد is the old/poetic 3rd‑person subjunctive/passive of گشت 'become' meaning 'becomes'. ز من is the prepositional phrase 'with me' or 'because of me' (ز here marks the object/cause).
segments.s208-g112: وزین is an adjective meaning 'weighty/dignified' (here 'dignified' or 'important'). نامور is 'renowned'. بی‌گناه is the descriptive adjective 'innocent'. انجمن is the noun 'assembly' or 'council'. Grammatically this is a noun phrase that specifies the cause or context ('from/through this dignified, renowned, innocent assembly').
segments.s208-g9: برادرش را = 'his brother' with را marking a definite direct object; کشته is the past participle / perfect meaning 'killed' (predicate). بر بی‌گناه is a prepositional phrase where بر (here 'against/upon') links the killing to the victim's innocence: poetically 'he struck down his brother, an innocent.' Word order is compressed and typical of epic Persian.
segments.s208-g10: به دشمن سپرده = 'entrusted/handed over to the enemy': به introduces the recipient and سپرده is a past participle/verb meaning 'entrusted/handed over.' نگین و کلاه follow as the direct objects (the things given). Note the typical Persian order 'to X [verb] Y and Z' without repeating را.
segments.s208-g11: همه یکسره دست کرده = 'all (of them) entirely/higgledy-piggledy having put (their) hand': the idiom دست کردن به + [root] means 'to take up / set one's hand to (something)'. کش here is a compressed root (from کشیدن) used poetically to mean 'to draw' (as in draw a sword). یکسره intensifies the immediacy ('all at once/completely').
segments.s208-g12: برفتند پیشش = 'they went/advanced before him' (برفتند is past 3pl of رفتن, پیشش = 'in his presence/before him'). پرستار فش is a descriptive nominal phrase: پرستار (literally 'worshipper/adorer' or 'one who attends to') combined with فش (oppression/pressure) functions as an epithet — poetically 'worshippers/servants of oppression' or 'oppressors'.
segments.s208-g13: بدیشان نگه کرد خسرو به خشم = literal order: 'toward them (بدیشان) he cast a look (نگه کرد), Khosrow (خسرو), in anger (به خشم)'. نگه کرد is a light-verb construction ('to make a gaze' = 'to look'); خسرو is the subject inserted in the middle for emphasis, and به خشم is an adverbial phrase indicating manner ('in anger').
segments.s208-g14: دلش پر ز درد و پر از خون دو چشم = 'his heart (دلش) full (پر) of pain (پر ز درد) and (و) his two eyes full (پر از) of blood (خون)'. Note the poetic alternation of the prepositions ز and از both meaning 'of/from' to mark 'full of'; پر is repeated to predicate both clauses, and دو چشم (two eyes) follows the phrase 'پر از خون' by stylistic inversion.
segments.s208-g15: به یزدان چنین گفت کای دادگر = 'to God (به یزدان) he thus said (چنین گفت): "O (کای) just one/righteous one (دادگر)".' چنین گفت is a formula meaning 'thus said'; کای is a vocative particle ('O') addressing the divine epithet دادگر.
segments.s208-g16: تو دادی مرا هوش و رای و هنر = 'you (تو) gave (دادی) me (مرا) intelligence (هوش), counsel/wise judgment (رای), and skill/arts (هنر)'. دادی is 2nd-person past; مرا is the accusative 'me' (tokenized here as م + را). The series هوش و رای و هنر are direct objects linked by و.
segments.s208-g129: چنین ننگ بر شاه ایران نبود is a full clause with the negated copula نبود at the end (a common classical Persian order: predicate before the verb). چنین here modifies ننگ as 'such' or 'that kind of', ننگ is the noun 'shame' and بر شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'upon the king of Iran' marking who bears the shame. The overall sense is 'there was not such a disgrace upon the Iranian king.'
segments.s208-g130: زمین پر ز خون دلیران نبود repeats the negative copula pattern: زمین 'land/earth' is the topic, پر ز خون 'full of blood' uses the poetic order پر ز (instead of modern پر از), and دلیران is the genitive 'of the brave'. نبود negates the whole: 'the land was not full of the brave ones' blood.'
segments.s208-g131: همه کوه پر خون گودرزیان: همه کوه 'the whole/entire mountain' (همه functioning as 'entire'), پر خون = 'full of blood' and گودرزیان in postposition as the genitive 'of the Gudarzis'. The noun phrase describes the mountain soaked with that clan's blood.
segments.s208-g132: به زنار خونین ببسته میان is a verbless clause built around the participle ببسته 'bound/tied'. به زنار خونین 'with/by a bloodstained girdle' marks the instrument or garment, and میان 'at the middle/waist' gives the location. The construction means 'they had bound a bloody girdle about their waists.' Note the older prefixal participle form ببسته.
segments.s208-g133: همان مرغ و ماهی بر ایشان بزار places the nominal actors first: همان مرغ و ماهی 'those very birds and fish'. بر ایشان 'toward them' marks the addressee/target, and بزار is an optative/imperative 'let/allow' introducing what follows. Together it is a wish: 'Let those birds and fish (toward them)...'.
segments.s208-g134: بگرید به دریا و بر کوهسار is the complement introduced by بزار: بگرید is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative 'may they weep/lament'. به دریا 'into/toward the sea' and بر کوهسار 'on the mountain' are locative phrases showing where the lamentation happens. The two locatives are coordinated by و.
segments.s208-g135: از ایران همه دشت تورانیان begins with از ایران 'from/throughout Iran' as a frame, then همه دشت 'every/plain (all the plains)' and تورانیان in postposition marks possession or association 'of the Turanians'. The phrase sets the scene: throughout Iran, on every plain belonging to (or filled with) Turanians...
segments.s208-g136: سر و دست و پایست و پشت و میان is a poetic catalogue of body-parts: سر 'heads', دست 'hands', پایست here functions as the noun-form 'feet' (classical orthography merges پا + است or writes پایست), then پشت 'backs' and میان 'middles/midsections'. The copula or verb is omitted (a common terseness in epic lists), implying these parts lay scattered across the plains.
segments.s208-g17: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'still/continually') placed before the predicate; شرم is the noun 'shame' used as the feeling; دارم is the 1st-person present 'I have' used idiomatically with a noun to mean 'I feel/I am (ashamed)'; من is the overt subject 'I' (optional in Persian, here emphasised); از is the preposition 'of/about' linking شرم to its object تو; کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s208-g18: تو is the subject 'you'; آگه‌تری is a comparative adjective formed with the comparative suffix -تری ('more aware/better informed') and stands after the subject (no copula is required in Persian); بی‌شک is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the predicate; از introduces the prepositional phrase that follows and چند و چون is an idiomatic paired expression meaning 'the hows and whys' or 'the details/circumstances' (چند = 'how much/how many', چون = 'how/why').
segments.s208-g19: وگرنه is the conditional conjunction 'otherwise'; بفرمودمی is an archaic/honorific 1st-person past/conditional form ('I would have commanded/said') — the -می here belongs to classical narrative/conditional patterns; تا here functions as 'up to/as many as' (classical use conveying extent).
segments.s208-g20: زدندی is a classical 3rd-person plural past form 'they struck/they would strike' used here as the verb in the subordinate clause governed by the previous conditional command; به is the preposition 'to/into/onto'; میدان is 'field/arena' and پیکار is 'battle' so میدان پیکار = 'field of battle'; دار is an older noun meaning 'gallows' (used here as a noun following the locative phrase), yielding a literal sense 'on/to the gallows' — in context the phrase describes violent punishment on the battlefield.
segments.s208-g21: تن طوس را is a definite object phrase ('the body of Tous') with را marking definiteness/object; دار is the noun 'gallows' (here indicating the place of execution); بودی is the past form 'was' used in this classical narrative to indicate a counterfactual/result; نشست is the verb 'sat' or 'took place' (here a stative/result verb describing how the body would have been placed or an event would have occurred). Word order is object — place — copula — verb, typical of poetic/classical Persian compressions.
segments.s208-g22: هرانکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever' (هرانکس = anyone who); که is the relativizer 'who/that'; با او means 'with him'; میان را literally 'the middle/interval' (میان را ببست = 'closed the middle' is an idiom for 'made peace/reconciled' or 'settled the matter'); ببست is the simple past 'he closed/tied', here used idiomatically for concluding or cutting off an affair.
segments.s208-g23: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' indicating cause; کین پدر is a genitive 'my father's hatred/vengeance' (کین = grudge/vengeance, پدر = father); بودم is the 1st-person past 'I was'; اندر خروش is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in an uproar/tumult' where اندر = 'in' and خروش = 'uproar'.
segments.s208-g24: دلی here denotes 'a heart' or 'inner feeling' as the object; داشتم is the 1st-person past 'I had'; پر is the adjective 'full (of)' that takes a following list introduced without a preposition; غم، درد و جوش are the nouns in the list — 'sorrow, pain, and agitation/ardor' — linked by و 'and'. The whole phrase is a simple past possession construction: 'I had a heart full of ...'.
segments.s208-g121: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تاج خور is a genitive phrase meaning 'the crown of the sun' (تاج = crown, خور = sun) with روشن ('bright') as a postpositive adjective. آمد پدید is a poetic verb sequence meaning 'came into view/appeared' — the verb elements are inverted compared with everyday prose (پدید آمد would be the more neutral order).
segments.s208-g122: سپیده is the subject 'the dawn'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خم کمان 'the curve/bend of the bow' is a compound noun phrase (خم 'bend', کمان 'bow'). بردمید is a poetic past-tense verb meaning 'sprang up/rose' (here used intransitively); the whole clause says the dawn rose from the bow's curve.
segments.s208-g123: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high military commander). بیامد is the past tense 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near the king' (به نزدیک = toward/near, شاه = king); together: 'the spahbed came near the king.'
segments.s208-g124: ابا او here functions as an archaic/poetic accompaniment marker equivalent to modern با او ('with him'); it introduces who accompanied the spahbed. بزرگان ایران سپاه lists those companions: 'the great men (بزرگان) of Iran and the army (سپاه)'. The nouns are put in apposition without a coordinating conjunction, a compact poetic order.
segments.s208-g125: بدیشان is an oblique pronoun phrase 'to them' (بی + ایشان). چنین is the adverb 'thus' or 'so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (third person); in this poetic line the verb precedes the subject for emphasis, so گفت شاه جهان reads 'thus said the king of the world.'
segments.s208-g126: که introduces the reported clause 'that...'. هرگز is the negator 'never'. پی کین is a noun phrase 'the track/trace of vengeance' (پی = track/trace, کین = vengeance). نگردد is the present/subjunctive form of شدن/گشتن with negative prefix ن- meaning 'does not become' or 'shall not be made'; نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. Word order places the verb before the adjective (poetic inversion): 'the trace of vengeance will never become hidden.'
segments.s208-g127: ز تور و ز سلم uses the preposition ز 'from' to mark source: 'from Tur and from Salm' (Tur and Salm are proper names). اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'arose' or 'came forth' and سخن is the thematic noun 'speech/talk'; the poetic order places آمد (verb) before its noun, giving 'talk arose.'
segments.s208-g128: ازان 'from that' links the spoken matter to what was just mentioned. کین پیشین is 'the earlier/former hatred' (کین = vengeance/hatred, پیشین = former). و رزم کهن adds 'and the ancient battle' (رزم = battle, کهن = ancient). These noun phrases are the content of the speech that 'arose' in the previous segment.
segments.s208-g81: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative phrase meaning 'consider/know thus' (چنان + دان 'consider'). که introduces a reported clause: کس بی‌بهانه نمرد literally 'someone without-cause did-not-die' — with کس in a negative context meaning 'no one' and نمرد a past negative form meaning 'did not die' (here a general assertion 'no one dies without cause').
segments.s208-g82: از این در سخنها is a figurative locution: از این در ('from this door') = 'concerning this matter/point' and سخنها (plural) = 'the words/remarks'. بباید is the impersonal modal 'one must/ought', and شمرد is the infinitive/verb 'to count; to reckon' — together 'this ought to be reckoned among the remarks on this matter.'
segments.s208-g83: و دیگر 'and another' introduces an additional point. کزان 'from which/from whom' links to the people just mentioned (those of bad feeling); بدگمان بد سپاه is a noun phrase 'ill‑disposed (people) [made] a bad host/army' — the verb is supplied by the following clause introduced with که.
segments.s208-g84: که here functions as 'for/since/that' introducing the explanation. فرخ برادر is an appositive noun phrase 'Farrokh, the brother'; نبد is an archaic negative past/copula meaning 'was not'; نزد شاه 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before/at the side of'). The clause explains why the host was bad: Farrokh was not before the king.
segments.s208-g85: همان طوس 'that same Tūs' identifies the person. تندست (from تندس) is a predicative adjective/noun meaning 'impetuous/hot‑tempered' and is coordinated with هشیار نیست: 'and (he) is not alert' — the negation نیست applies to هشیار. The clause states two character traits of Tūs.
segments.s208-g86: و دیگر که introduces another point: جان پسر خوار نیست literally 'the son's life is not despised' — a negative copular clause meaning 'the young man's life is not to be scorned/it is not worthless.' Here جان (life) is the patient, پسر 'the son/young man', خوار 'despised/worthless', and نیست negates the predicate.
segments.s208-g87: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. در پیش او 'in/at his presence' = 'before him'; کشته شد is the passive perfect 'was killed' (کشته + شد as auxiliary), and ریونیز (a name) is the patient placed after the verb by poetic inversion. The clause: 'When Rivoniz was killed before him...'.
segments.s208-g88: زرسپ آن جوان سرافراز نیز is a noun phrase appended to the prior clause with an implied verb: 'Zarasp, that proud young man, also (was killed).' آن جوان سرافراز is an appositive 'that young, honored (or triumphant) man', and نیز 'also' indicates the parallel fate.
segments.s208-g97: همی before a past verb (کرد) marks a continuous or iterative past action — “kept/was continually.” آفرین کردن is a verbal phrase meaning “to praise” (آفرین here is the object/act of praise, an acclamation). بر + noun (بر شهریار) marks the target of the praise (“upon/to the king”). کرد is the past tense main verb “did/made” (here, ‘praised’).
segments.s208-g98: که introduces a reported clause (that/which). نوشه is a noun ‘a draught/portion’ (a poetic word for a sip/potion). بدی here is anomalous in the provided senses: in classical Persian this is most likely an archaic verb form (2nd-person past, ‘you gave’ or ‘you caused to drink’) or a poetic usage meaning ‘took/gave (a draught)’; the senses list supplied (meanings like ‘evil’) does not match this verbal use, so it is annotated as uncertain. تا + بود روزگار = ‘as long as time/day (literally: until the time existed)’ with بود as past ‘was/lasted’ and روزگار ‘time/fate’, completing the temporal clause (“while/so long as the days lasted”).
segments.s208-g99: This is an optative/blessing clause ending in باد (‘may’). زمین بنده is an equational phrase: ‘let the earth be a servant’ (بنده = servant). تاج و تخت تو enumerates honors that belong to you (‘your crown and throne’); تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun and باد at the end makes the whole phrase a wish (‘may the earth be a servant, (may) your crown and throne (be yours)’).
segments.s208-g100: Another optative clause with باد final: فلک مایه فر و بخت تو باد = ‘May the heavens be the source (مایه) of your فر (royal glory) and of your بخت (fortune).’ Here مایه = ‘cause/source’, فر = ‘royal glory’, and فلک (heavens) is the subject/agent of that blessing; the coordinating و links فر and بخت.
segments.s208-g101: منم is emphatic ‘I am’ (present identification). The phrase دل پر از غم = ‘a heart full of grief’ uses پر + از to mean ‘full of’. ز (poetic variant of از) before کردار gives the cause/source ‘from/because of my deeds’; کردار خویش = ‘my own deeds’ (خویش is a reflexive/possessive element here, tokenized as خوی + ش). Word order is subject-identifying then a verbless predication describing the speaker’s heart.
segments.s208-g102: به غم بسته جان را is a compact clause: به here means ‘in/with’ (marking the condition), بسته (past participle/adjective) = ‘bound/tied’, و جان را is the definite object marked by را (‘the life/soul’). ز تیمار خویش = ‘by/from my own care’ (ز = from/because of, تیمار = care/solicitude, خوی + ش = own/his). The sense is ‘I have bound my life with sorrow because of my own caring/concern.’
segments.s208-g103: همان نیز = ‘likewise/also’ (همان here = ‘the same/that way’ and نیز = ‘also’). جانم = ‘my life/soul’ (possessive suffix on جان). پر از شرم = ‘full of shame’ (پر + از pattern). شاه at the end is a vocative address (‘O king’). The clause is verbless/descriptive: ‘likewise, my life is full of shame, O king.’
segments.s208-g104: This is a pair of parallel nominal phrases: زبان پر ز پوزش = ‘(my) tongue full of apology’ (ز = ‘of/from’, پوزش = apology/excuse). روان پر گناه = ‘(my) soul full of sin’ (روان here = ‘soul/spirit’, پر = ‘full (of)’, گناه = ‘sin’). The two noun phrases are placed together for rhetorical accumulation (‘my tongue full of apologies, my soul full of sin’).
segments.s208-g33: کنون (adverb) + لاجرم (adverb) precede the noun phrase for emphasis: “now, necessarily/inevitably.” کردگار سپهر is a poetic epithet (“Creator / Lord of the sky/heavens”); the two nouns sit together as a title without an explicit ezāfe, typical of epic diction.
segments.s208-g34: ز … و ز … marks the sources (“from / because of … and from …”). ببرید is the classical perfect/past form of بردن here meaning “took away / cut off.” مهر is the object “affection, favour.” Word order is poetic (source phrases before the verb).
segments.s208-g35: بد آمد is an idiom meaning “(it) turned out badly / evil befell.” به گودرزیان is a dative phrase “for/to the Gudarzis.” بر ز طوس gives the cause—poetic sequence of prepositions—“on account of / because of Tūs.”
segments.s208-g36: که introduces the following optative/wish clause. نفرین بر او باد is a standard formula: نفرین “a curse,” بر او “upon him,” باد is the optative particle “may it be.” The trailing و بر پیل و کوس extends the curse to the elephant and the drum.
segments.s208-g37: همی here is an emphatic/continuative adverb (“thereupon/indeed”). خلعت و پندها are the direct objects (“robe(s) of honour and admonitions”). دادمش is a past-tense verb with the enclitic pronoun -مش “him” attached: “I gave him (them).” Object(s) precede the verb in poetic order.
segments.s208-g38: به جنگ is a prepositional goal “to/into battle.” برادر is the person involved (“(his) brother”); in combination with فرستادمش (“I sent him”) it means “I sent him to fight his brother / to the battle against his brother.” فرستادمش is 1st-person past with enclitic -مش “him.”
segments.s208-g39: This is an optative-negative wish: the personal name (جهانگیر) is followed by چون “like” and two names (طوس نوذر) and closes with مباد “may it not be.” Thus: “May Jahāngīr not be like Tūs (or Nowzar).” The order places the comparison inside the clause before the negative optative verb.
segments.s208-g40: چنو (archaic/poetic ‘such / as’) modifies پهلوان “champion, hero.” پیش لشکر is a locative/prepositional phrase “before/in front of the army/host.” مباد again is the negative optative “may there not be,” so the whole clause says “may there not be such a champion before the army.”
segments.s208-g153: فراوانش is فراوان + enclitic -ش (him/it) and here adverbially qualifies the praise: “lavishly/much to him.” بستود is a past-tense verb (هِٔ ستودن) “he praised.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بنواختش is بنواخت + -ش (him): بنواختن here is poetic for “to sound/strike (an instrument), to play for someone” — so the clause means “he praised him greatly and played (music) for him.”
segments.s208-g154: بسی is an adverb meaning “much/many” (intensifier). خلعت is a gift/robe of honour, and و links to the next noun. نیکوی is an adjective “good/excellent” modifying the following noun implicitly (here describing garments/honours). ساختش is ساخت + -ش (he made for him); ساختن in past here means “made/caused (for him)” — together: “he made for him many robes of honour and fine (garments).”
segments.s208-g155: بدو is the prepositional/enclitic phrase meaning “to him” (he said to him). گفت here is the past of گفتن (‘said’), but the listed noun senses do not match the verbal usage, so no listed sense fits (see senses map). کاندر is a fused preposition+pronoun meaning “in/within” (in which / in this). جهان = “the world/this life.” رنج من = “my suffering”; رنج is a direct object governed by کاندر جهان (i.e. “in the world of my suffering”).
segments.s208-g156: تو is the second‑person pronoun “you.” بردی is the simple past of بردن “you took/carried/received” (here: ‘you have borne/taken’). و is “and.” بی‌بهری (بی‌ + بهری) means “without benefit / deprived of advantage.” از is the preposition “from/of.” گنج من = “my treasure” (گنج = treasure; من = mine/my). The clause states that he took the speaker’s suffering yet derived no benefit from his treasure.
segments.s208-g157: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning “one must not / it should not” (negative obligation). که introduces the content clause (“that/that…”). بی رای تو = “without your counsel/consent” (بی = without, رای = opinion/counsel, تو = your/you). پیل و کوس = “elephant and drum” (typical military paraphernalia); the two nouns are coordinated by و. The whole segment sets up the thing that must not be done without the addressee’s advice.
segments.s208-g158: سوی جنگ = “toward battle” (سوی = toward, جنگ = battle). راند is the past of راندن “drove/sent/led” (here: ‘sent/put in motion toward’). سپهدار is the title “commander” and طوس is the name “Tūs” (here the heroic commander); Persian classical prose/verse often places the subject after verb, so راند سپهدار طوس = “the commander Tous led/sent.”
segments.s208-g159: به تندی is an adverbial phrase “in haste / with haste” (به + تندی). مکن is the negative imperative “do not.” سهمگین describes the severity “dreadful/grave.” کار خرد literally “deed of wisdom” — خرد here means “wisdom,” so the phrase warns against a rash but serious action that affects wise affairs; word order places the negation before the object: “do not (commit) a dreadful deed of wisdom” (i.e. don’t rashly attempt a grave matter of counsel).
segments.s208-g160: که is a subordinating particle here (“so that/that” introducing the following wish or reason). روشن‌روان is an epithet “bright-souled / clear-minded” describing بهرام. باد is the optative particle “may” (a fixed poetic use). گرد is a verbal element from گردیدن ‘to become/go about’ used here in a copulative/optative construction: روشن‌روان باد بهرام گرد = “May Bahram be (the) bright‑souled one” (a short blessing/wish). Word order is poetic: the wish-particle باد precedes the name and is followed by گرد as part of the optative form.
segments.s208-g49: ز is the poetic ablative/prepositional particle 'from/because of' introducing the cause. خون برادر is a noun‑noun genitive construction ('the blood of (his) brother') with no ezafe (typical of older/poetic Persian). به کین پدر places کین (vengeance/hatred) with the preposition به linking it to پدر ('the father's vengeance') and functions as a second causal/attendant phrase that combines with ز-clause to give the reasons for the following state.
segments.s208-g50: همی is the older/epic continuous marker (marks ongoing or habitual action). گشت is a past/aorist form of 'to turn/become' used here with همی to mean 'kept turning/was twisting.' پیچان is a participial/adjectival form 'twisting/winding' describing the state of جگر, and then و خسته جگر coordinates a second descriptive state ('and weary was the jigar'); note the somewhat inverted/poetic order where the verb/adjective cluster precedes the noun (جگر) they qualify.
segments.s208-g51: سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the army' as the direct object of both verbs that follow. همه خوار کرد is a causative phrase: کرد (past 'made') takes خوار ('despised/despicable') as the result state — 'made the whole army despicable' = 'humiliated the whole army.' و براند connects a second verb (راند) that shares the same object; براند here functions as 'drove away/routed (them)'.
segments.s208-g52: ز مژگان is an ablative/agentive phrase 'from (his) eyelashes' (poetic image). همی again marks continuous or habitual action. خون is the direct object and به رخ marks the target ('upon the face'); برفشاند (causative of پاشیدن) means 'to scatter/spatter upon' — together: 'he would spatter blood upon his face from his eyelashes.' Word order is somewhat poetic (ablative phrase fronted, then continuous marker plus object and target, then verb).
segments.s208-g53: در here is the noun 'door/gate.' بار دادن is a verbal noun + infinitive sense ('giving/bringing of a load/gift' — i.e., presenting tribute or gifts) that situates the action; بر ایشان ('against/on them') marks who is affected. ببست is the past tense 'he closed/shut.' Put together the clause means 'he shut the gate on them (when they came to give tribute),' with the subject implicit (third‑person).
segments.s208-g54: روانش is روان ('soul/spirit') plus the third‑person possessive suffix -ش 'his soul.' به مرگ برادر is a causal/occasional phrase 'at/by the death of (his) brother.' بخست is the past form 'shut/closed.' The clause reads metaphorically: his spirit 'closed' (was struck/withdrew) upon his brother's death; verb agreement is third‑person singular implied by the suffix on the subject.
segments.s208-g55: بزرگان ایران is a genitive phrase 'the great men of Iran' (بزرگان = plural noun 'great ones'). به ماتم شد‑ند uses به + noun to indicate state/result ('into mourning') and شدند is the third‑person plural past of شدن ('became/were'), agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s208-g56: دلیران ('the brave ones') is the plural subject. به درگاه رستم is a prepositional phrase marking motion/toward ('to the court/threshold of Rostam'); درگاه Rِستم is a genitive construction where رستم names whose court it is. شدند again is third‑person plural past of شدن and here conveys 'they went/arrived' or 'they took their place' at Rostam's court.
segments.s208-g73: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. شد is the past of شدن 'become/turn' (here 'turned'). روی گیتی is a poetic noun phrase 'the face/surface of the world'. ز + خورشید expresses cause or source ('from the sun'), and زرد is a predicate adjective attached to the subject: 'the world's face became yellow because of the sun.'
segments.s208-g74: به خم اندر آمد is a short verb phrase meaning 'came into the bend/curve' (به ... آمد + اندر for in/into), a poetic way to say the night descended into the curve of the horizon. شب لاژورد is a noun plus color/quality: 'the lapis/ultramarine night' (lažvard naming the deep blue color).
segments.s208-g75: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmātan). بیامد is a past form 'came' (bi‑amadan, here simple past). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' indicating approach; the verb governs that phrase.
segments.s208-g76: ببوسید is the third‑person past 'he kissed' (subject omitted, understood from previous clause). خاک از در پیشگاه is a genitive/locative cluster: 'the earth/ground (خاک) of/from the gate (در) of the presence/court (پیشگاه)' — i.e. 'kissed the ground at the court's gate.' Note the poetic ordering where the object precedes its modifying prepositional phrase.
segments.s208-g77: چنین گفت introduces reported speech 'thus said'. The pronominal مر is dative 'to me' (indirect object). شاه را uses the accusative marker را with 'king' (the referent of the reported remark), and پیلتن is the subject/name 'Pilten' placed after the clause — a flexible, topicalized word order common in older poetic Persian.
segments.s208-g78: که بادا is a conventional optative formula 'that/may it be' (introducing a wish). سرت is 'your head' (possessive form), برتر از is a comparative 'higher/superior than', and انجمن is 'the assembly' — together forming the blessing 'May your head be higher than the assembly.'
segments.s208-g79: به خواهشگری آمدم is 'I came for entreaty' or 'I came as a petitioner'; آمدم is first person past 'I came'. نزد شاه means 'in the presence of the king' or 'before the king' (نزد here marks being in someone's presence).
segments.s208-g80: همان 'the same' picks up identity ('the very same person'). از پی + طوس is a poetic compound 'after/following Tūs' (از پی = in the wake of / following), and بهر سپاه gives the purpose/benefit 'for the army' (بهر = for/on behalf of). Together: 'the same who followed Tūs for the army.'
segments.s208-g57: به پوزش is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('by way of excuse' / 'as an apology') modifying the reported statement. که introduces a content clause ('that ...'). این بودنی کار is a noun phrase: این ('this') modifies بودنی (a nominalized participle/description meaning 'that‑which‑is' or 'necessary/inevitable'), with کار ('affair/deed') as the head; بود is the past copula ('was').
segments.s208-g58: This clause gives the content of the excuse. که again begins the subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'). را is the definite/object/topic marker attached to the (omitted) person or thing being targeted. بود is the past copula ('was'). آهنگ رزم is a compound meaning 'the intention/plan of battle' (آهنگ = intention/aim, رزم = battle) and فرود here is the agent/name (a proper name); word order is poetic: verb/copula follows the clause, and agent/name follows the nominal phrase.
segments.s208-g59: بدانگه is an adverb meaning 'thereupon' or 'at that moment'. کجا is the locative/interrogative 'where' introducing the location clause. کشته شد is a passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed/slain') + شد as the past auxiliary 'was/killed'. پور طوس is a compound name/title ('son of Tūs' / Pūr‑Tūs) placed after the verb in the poem's inverted order.
segments.s208-g60: سر سرکشان is a genitive/possessive grouping: سر ('head') with سرکشان (the 'peepers'/lookouts; poetically formed) specifying whose head. خیره گشت is a verbal phrase 'became astonished/stunned' (خیره = astonished/staring, گشت = became). از introduces the cause ('because of' / 'from') and فسوس names the cause ('whispering/insidious talk'). Word order again follows poetry: possessor after head and verb after predicate adverb.
segments.s208-g61: همان نیز is an emphatic 'likewise/also'. داماد او is the possessed noun 'his son‑in‑law' (داماد 'son‑in‑law' + possessive pronoun او 'his'), with the proper/name ریونیز following in apposition; poetic ordering places modifiers and apposition around the noun.
segments.s208-g62: نبود is the negative past copula 'was not' (negated بودن). از introduces a source/characteristic ('of/from' or 'in the way of'), بد بخت is a compound meaning 'ill‑fated / unfortunate' (بد = bad, بخت = fortune), مانند چیز is a simile 'like a thing'—together the clause idiomatically states that he was nothing but ill‑fated (i.e., was an unfortunate man).
segments.s208-g63: که introduces a subordinate clause (here giving content: 'that / for'). دانست is the simple past 'he knew' or 'he learned'. نام و نژاد فرود is the compound object: نام ('name') and نژاد ('lineage') bound to the proper name فرود (Farud) placed after them in apposition — standard poetic order.
segments.s208-g64: کجا here functions adverbially ('how / in what way' or 'whereupon') pointing to the effect; شاه را دل is a topicalised possessive phrase meaning 'the king's heart' (شاه را uses را to topicalise the possessor). بخواهد is the present/subjunctive stem used to express desire ('would/will want'); شخود is a noun meaning 'self‑conceit / boastful pride' — together: 'how the king's heart would desire pride.'
